meta.title: NYC Gov for Builders
meta.tagline: A curated, encyclopedic map of NYC government's data landscape — so a new technologist can contribute in hours, not weeks.
meta.note: Source descriptions are ground truth from the product brief (§6), researched and accurate as of June 2026. Every URL below was checked to resolve before shipping. Time-sensitive facts (officeholders, figures, bill numbers) are deliberately omitted or kept as maintainable examples.

categories.name: City Council
categories.blurb: Legislation, hearings, votes — the official record and the independent tools that make it legible.

categories.name: Community Boards
categories.blurb: The hyper-local layer: district agendas, land-use review, and what neighbors are actually saying.

categories.name: NYC Government Operations
categories.blurb: Performance, open data, procurement, planning, and the day-to-day machinery of the city.

categories.name: Spending, rules, records & context
categories.blurb: Money, FOIL, agency rules, civic-tech tooling, and accountability journalism.

audiences.name: Technologist
audiences.note: developer / data analyst / designer — wants APIs, formats, and gotchas

audiences.name: New gov employee
audiences.note: needs the map of who-owns-what without the jargon

audiences.name: CBO / nonprofit staff
audiences.note: needs the same map, plain-language, self-serve

problems.label: Understand or track a bill
problems.blurb: Find a piece of Council legislation, read it in plain terms, see its status, sponsors, and what was said at the hearing.

problems.label: Track a new development near you
problems.blurb: Follow a land-use action from zoning context through community-board review, the public hearings, and the Council vote.

problems.label: Help someone get benefits or a job
problems.blurb: Screen for programs, locate the right centers and agencies, and track policy or budget changes that affect services.

problems.label: Analyze 311 / spot complaint patterns
problems.blurb: Pull service-request data to find patterns by neighborhood, complaint type, or agency response time.

problems.label: Find who holds a contract
problems.blurb: See which vendors hold city contracts, what's being procured right now, and how much is actually being spent.

problems.label: Get up to speed on an agency
problems.blurb: Build a fast, rounded picture of an agency: performance, money, independent analysis, public statements, and what's coming.

problems.label: Advocate for a local issue
problems.blurb: Document a problem on your block, prove a pattern with data, and bring it to the people who can act on it.

problems.label: Understand records & interagency data
problems.blurb: Learn how agencies share data, how to file a FOIL request, and where proposed rules are published for comment.

sources.name: Legistar
sources.whatItIs: The Council's system of record: bills (“Intros”), resolutions, the hearing calendar, committee agendas, minutes, votes, and webcasts.
sources.bestFor: Authoritative status and the official documents behind a piece of legislation.
sources.watchOutFor: Dense, dated UI. Agendas and minutes rarely capture what was actually said — use citymeetings.nyc for that.
sources.dataAccessNote: Web/scrape for most users; a public Council Legislative API exists as the data layer (see the API entry).

sources.name: citymeetings.nyc
sources.whatItIs: Same-day, AI-with-human-oversight transcription of Council and Charter Revision Commission meetings, broken into skimmable, citable chapters linked to the video and transcript. Free, with a newsletter.
sources.bestFor: Finding what was actually said in a long hearing — fast.
sources.watchOutFor: Occasional AI transcription errors. Covers the Council and the Charter Revision Commission only.

sources.name: intro.nyc
sources.whatItIs: A fast, readable front-end for Council legislation built on the Council's public Legislative API, with clean shareable URLs (e.g. intro.nyc/0139-2026), status, sponsors, and reports.
sources.bestFor: Looking up, reading, and sharing a specific bill.
sources.watchOutFor: A reading layer over the official data — confirm anything decisive against Legistar.
sources.dataAccessNote: Built on the public Council Legislative API; bill pages follow the intro.nyc/<file-number> pattern.

sources.name: Council Legislative API
sources.whatItIs: The public legislative data API that powers tools like intro.nyc — programmatic access to bills, sponsors, statuses, and reports.
sources.bestFor: Building on Council legislation data directly instead of scraping Legistar.
sources.watchOutFor: We link to Legistar as the official entry point; confirm the current API base URL from the Council before integrating. Treat the exact endpoint as a maintainable detail.

sources.name: laws.council.nyc.gov
sources.whatItIs: The Council's newer beta legislative site, with email alerts on legislation.
sources.bestFor: Email alerts on bills (when available).
sources.watchOutFor: TODO — this URL did not resolve during our checks (connection refused). Flagged as unverified; confirm it is live before relying on it.

sources.name: Community Board websites
sources.whatItIs: Per-board calendars, agendas and minutes, committee structure, district needs statements and budget requests, ULURP reviews, and local permits.
sources.bestFor: A district's local agenda and how to testify.
sources.watchOutFor: Quality and freshness vary widely from board to board. Web-only and inconsistent.
sources.dataAccessNote: Each board's URL lives under nyc.gov (e.g. nyc.gov/site/manhattancb…). Linked here is the citywide directory of boards.

sources.name: Block Party
sources.whatItIs: A public archive of community-board meeting transcripts from YouTube captions (roughly 2015 onward), topic-tagged using a taxonomy from DCP Community District Profiles, with highlights, an address→district lookup, and a per-board weekly email.
sources.bestFor: Searching what boards across the city are actually discussing.
sources.watchOutFor: Depends on boards posting captioned video; accuracy is bounded by caption quality.
sources.dataAccessNote: Web interface plus a structured transcript dataset.
sources.updateCadence: As boards post captioned video; weekly per-board email.

sources.name: DMMR — Dynamic Mayor's Management Report
sources.whatItIs: The interactive version of the Charter-mandated Mayor's Management Report: 1,000+ performance indicators across ~45 agencies, with multi-year comparisons, all downloadable.
sources.bestFor: Answering “how is this agency performing?” with official numbers.
sources.watchOutFor: Indicators are what agencies choose to report; read them as performance signals, not the whole story.
sources.updateCadence: Tied to the MMR/PMMR reporting cycle.

sources.name: NYC Open Data
sources.whatItIs: The central catalog of thousands of datasets with Socrata APIs, maps, and visualizations — the backbone for civic hackers.
sources.bestFor: Raw data for analysis and building.
sources.watchOutFor: Dataset freshness and documentation vary by owning agency; check each dataset's update cadence and column dictionary.
sources.dataAccessNote: Full Socrata API plus bulk download on essentially every dataset.

sources.name: PASSport / PASSport Public
sources.whatItIs: NYC's end-to-end procurement platform. PASSport Public (2022) lets anyone browse vendors, solicitations (RFx), and contracts.
sources.bestFor: Seeing who holds contracts and what's being procured.
sources.watchOutFor: Procurement-stage data; pair with Checkbook to see what was actually spent.
sources.dataAccessNote: Public transparency portal; pair with Checkbook NYC for spend.

sources.name: City Record Online (CROL)
sources.whatItIs: The city's daily journal of record: procurement notices (≥ $100k), public hearings, rulemaking, property dispositions, and official notices. Free email alerts.
sources.bestFor: Official notices and deadlines.
sources.watchOutFor: Notice-dense and formal; skim with alerts rather than reading cover to cover.
sources.dataAccessNote: Web plus email alerts; an Open Data mirror also exists.

sources.name: NYC Planning (ZoLa & tools)
sources.whatItIs: City Planning's hub and tools: ZoLa (the zoning & land-use map), Community District Profiles, Population FactFinder, and the Capital Planning / Projects portal.
sources.bestFor: Zoning, land use, demographics, and neighborhood context.
sources.watchOutFor: Several distinct tools under one department — know which one answers your question.
sources.dataAccessNote: Interactive maps plus downloadable datasets and APIs.

sources.name: NYC Capital Projects Dashboard
sources.whatItIs: Citywide construction projects (bridges, sewers, sanitation, buildings) with total cost, phase, and projected completion; budget pulled from FMS and agency schedules.
sources.bestFor: Tracking a specific capital project.
sources.watchOutFor: Refreshed roughly three times a year — not real-time.
sources.dataAccessNote: Dashboard plus an Open Data dictionary.

sources.whatItIs: Report problems (litter, potholes, noise), request services, and look up agencies. Every request becomes a 311 Service Request in Open Data.
sources.bestFor: Residents reporting and tracking issues; analysts spotting complaint patterns.
sources.watchOutFor: 311 data reflects what's reported, not what exists — reporting rates differ by neighborhood.
sources.dataAccessNote: 311 Service Requests is one of the most-used Open Data feeds (full API).

sources.name: NYC Interagency Data-Sharing Agreements
sources.whatItIs: A reference and visualization of how NYC agencies share data via MOUs, and the legal infrastructure behind it.
sources.bestFor: Understanding the interagency data-sharing landscape.
sources.watchOutFor: An independent reference; confirm specific agreements against the agencies involved.

sources.name: Checkbook NYC
sources.whatItIs: The Comptroller's transparency site for city spending, contracts, budget, revenue, and payroll.
sources.bestFor: Seeing what was actually spent — the natural pair to PASSport's procurement view.
sources.watchOutFor: Big, multi-dimensional data; be precise about fiscal year and spending category.
sources.updateCadence: Regular, tied to city financial reporting.

sources.name: Independent Budget Office (IBO)
sources.whatItIs: Nonpartisan analysis of the city's budget and finances.
sources.bestFor: An independent read on the money, separate from the administration's framing.
sources.watchOutFor: Analysis and reports, not a live data feed — publication-paced.

sources.name: OpenRecords (FOIL)
sources.whatItIs: The central portal for filing Freedom of Information Law (FOIL) requests across city agencies.
sources.bestFor: Getting records that aren't already published.
sources.watchOutFor: Response times vary widely by agency; scope requests narrowly.

sources.name: NYC Rules
sources.whatItIs: Proposed and adopted agency rules, with public hearings and a way to comment on proposed rules.
sources.bestFor: Tracking and weighing in on agency rulemaking.
sources.watchOutFor: Rulemaking is procedural and time-boxed — comment windows close.

sources.name: WeGov.NYC / NYC Databook
sources.whatItIs: A civic nonprofit that stitches Open Data into readable agency, budget, and capital profiles.
sources.bestFor: A builder-friendly, human-readable profile of an agency or the budget.
sources.watchOutFor: Independent synthesis; trace decisive figures back to the official source.

sources.name: BetaNYC
sources.whatItIs: A civic-technology organization and community offering civic resources, district and boundary lookups, and Open Data programming.
sources.bestFor: Tooling, boundary/district lookups, and finding the civic-tech community — both a source and an audience.
sources.watchOutFor: Community-run; offerings evolve.

sources.name: The City
sources.whatItIs: Nonprofit local news covering NYC government with context and accountability reporting.
sources.bestFor: Understanding the story and stakes around a decision.
sources.watchOutFor: Journalism, not a primary record — follow through to the underlying source.

sources.name: Gotham Gazette
sources.whatItIs: A pioneering, nonpartisan online publication covering NYC and State government.
sources.bestFor: Background and explainer-style coverage of city government.
sources.watchOutFor: Stale-prone: the site ceased regular updates in 2023 and now functions mainly as an archive. Treat as historical context, not current news.

sources.name: City Limits
sources.whatItIs: Nonprofit journalism covering NYC policy, housing, and the communities affected by it.
sources.bestFor: Policy and community-impact context.
sources.watchOutFor: Journalism, not a primary record — follow through to the underlying source.

sources.name: NYC Charter & Administrative Code
sources.owner: City of New York (published via American Legal)
sources.whatItIs: The Charter (the city's constitution) and the Administrative Code — the legal framework that defines what agencies are and what they may do.
sources.bestFor: Grounding a question in the actual law.
sources.watchOutFor: Legal text; the host blocks automated checks, so this link was confirmed as the canonical code library but not auto-verified — it opens normally in a browser.

sources.name: ACCESS NYC
sources.whatItIs: The city's official benefits-screening site — find help with food, money, housing, and work — with ACCESS HRA as the application portal for many programs.
sources.bestFor: Screening someone for the programs they may be eligible for.
sources.watchOutFor: Screening tool, not a determination; eligibility is decided by the administering agency. Don't over-claim eligibility from the screener.
sources.dataAccessNote: Web tool; ACCESS HRA (a069-access.nyc.gov) is the linked application portal.
